Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Condenses one p-coumaroyl-CoA with three malonyl-CoA molecules to form chalcone, the first committed step of flavonoid biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Supplies core flavonoid precursor pool for anthocyanin biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: First committed enzyme of flavonoid biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Pigmentation in flowers and fruit; associated with protection against certain cancers, cardiovascular diseases and other chronic human disorders; antioxidant-related benefits
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CHS is identified as the first committed enzyme of flavonoid biosynthesis. 2. Its activity provides the entry step required for anthocyanin formation. 3. It is presented as part of the core pathway summarized in the review, not as a focal experimental target in a new study.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for anthocyanin biosynthetic entry and thus for anthocyanin accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Review summary based on prior pathway elucidation
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Background/core pathway gene summarized in review; no single accession or species-specific experiment provided in this article.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Isomerizes chalcone to flavanone in the anthocyanin/flavonoid pathway.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms immediate intermediate toward anthocyanin biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CHI is one of the further enzymes required for anthocyanidin synthesis. 2. It acts in the shared flavonoid pathway upstream of colored anthocyanidins. 3. The paper includes it in pathway overview rather than as a specific engineering target.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports anthocyanin formation by enabling early flavonoid conversion steps.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Review summary based on prior pathway elucidation
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: No species-specific accession or validation in this review.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydroxylates flavanones to dihydroflavonols required for anthocyanin formation.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms immediate precursor pool for anthocyanidin synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. F3H is listed among the enzymes required to synthesize anthocyanidins. 2. A chrysanthemum F3H promoter was used to drive transgenic F3'5'H expression in flower engineering. 3. The enzyme itself is a core pathway step but not the main engineering target in the cited examples.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes anthocyanin biosynthesis by providing dihydroflavonol intermediates.
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Chrysanthemum F3H promoter (used as promoter in chrysanthemum engineering example; inducibility not stated)
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Review summary and cited transgenic engineering examples
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Promoter use is described in chrysanthemum transformation figures; not a direct validation of F3H coding function in this review.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces dihydroflavonols to leucoanthocyanidins en route to anthocyanidins.
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: Dihydroflavonols, including dihydromyricetin in some species
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Late biosynthetic gene / downstream core step
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Not established; substrate specificity can determine branch output
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. DFR is required for anthocyanidin synthesis. 2. Host-species DFR substrate preference can limit delphinidin production after introducing F3'5'H. 3. Replacing endogenous DFR with petunia DFR improved engineering of blue-ish flowers because petunia/iris DFR can prefer trihydroxylated dihydromyricetin.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Determines whether hydroxylated dihydroflavonol intermediates are efficiently converted toward delphinidin-based anthocyanins.
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Petunia sp.; Iris sp.; Viridiplantae sp.
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Dianthus caryophyllus; Rosa sp.; Chrysanthemum morifolium; Viridiplantae sp.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transgenic replacement/heterologous expression summarized from cited studies
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: DFR substrate specificity is emphasized as a key species-dependent engineering constraint.

Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms anthocyanidin precursors of anthocyanins
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ANS/LDOX is one of the enzymes required to synthesize colored anthocyanidins. 2. It is part of the conserved core biosynthetic pathway summarized in the review. 3. It is discussed as an MBW-regulated late biosynthetic gene in many systems.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for formation of colored anthocyanidins and thus anthocyanins.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Review summary based on prior pathway elucidation
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Also referred to as LDOX in the review.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydroxylates the B-ring to direct formation of cyanidin-type anthocyanidins.
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: Anthocyanin/flavonoid pathway intermediates lacking the 3'-hydroxyl group
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directs flux toward cyanidin branch and competes with delphinidin branch engineering
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. F3'H forms cyanidin by B-ring hydroxylation. 2. In chrysanthemum engineering, endogenous F3'H competes with introduced F3'5'H. 3. Low endogenous F3'H activity was advantageous for delphinidin production in engineered chrysanthemums.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes cyanidin-type anthocyanin formation and can reduce delphinidin accumulation when competing with F3'5'H.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Comparative transgenic engineering observations summarized from cited studies
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Named as flavonoid 3' hydroxylase in text; competition with F3'5'H is a key engineering consideration.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydroxylates the flavonoid B-ring at the 3' and 5' positions to form intermediates leading to delphinidin.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms branch precursor for delphinidin-based anthocyanins
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key branch-switch enzyme for blue/purple delphinidin production
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Pigmentation, especially blue-purple coloration; dietary antioxidant/health-associated functions
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. F3'5'H is the major determinant for delphinidin biosynthesis and blue-purple coloration. 2. Silencing F3'5'H can shift color from blue to red. 3. Introducing F3'5'H from Petunia, Viola, or Campanula into carnation, rose, and chrysanthemum enabled delphinidin production and blue/blue-violet flowers. 4. Source-dependent F3'5'H enzymes differ in their ability to compete with endogenous F3'H in chrysanthemum.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes delphinidin accumulation and blue-purple flower color.
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Petunia sp.; Viola sp.; Campanula sp.
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Dianthus caryophyllus; Rosa sp.; Chrysanthemum morifolium
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Chrysanthemum F3H promoter (used to express Campanula F3'5'H in chrysanthemum)
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transgenic expression; gene silencing; flower color and anthocyanin profiling
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: One of the most important engineering genes discussed in the review; success can depend on endogenous F3'H and DFR specificity.

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Flavonol branch competing with anthocyanin biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Competes with anthocyanin branch and provides intermolecular co-pigments
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Pigmentation and stability effects through co-pigmentation
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. FLS synthesizes flavonols from the shared flavonoid pathway. 2. Inhibition of FLS in lisianthus resulted in redder flowers. 3. The effect was attributed to inhibition of intermolecular co-pigmentation of anthocyanins by flavonols.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Reducing FLS decreases flavonol co-pigments and shifts flower color toward red.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene inhibition/silencing summarized from cited study
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Affects anthocyanin display indirectly through co-pigmentation rather than anthocyanin backbone synthesis.

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Proanthocyanidin biosynthesis competing with anthocyanin accumulation
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces anthocyanidins to epicatechin precursors for proanthocyanidin synthesis.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Competes with anthocyanin accumulation by removing anthocyanidins
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Seed coat pigmentation; visual marker potential
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ANR removes anthocyanidins to supply epicatechin for proanthocyanidin synthesis. 2. Inhibiting ANR in soybean seed coat caused accumulation of red cyanidin-based anthocyanins. 3. The resulting seed color was proposed as a marker for transgenic soybean lines.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: ANR inhibition increases anthocyanin accumulation in seed coat and produces red seeds.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene inhibition/silencing summarized from cited study
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Can be used to engineer a visible transgenic seed marker through red anthocyanin accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Illustrates competition between anthocyanin and proanthocyanidin pathways.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Adds acyl groups to anthocyanin sugar residues at positions including 5 and 3' in gentian anthocyanins.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Required for storage/modification of target product
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Blue coloration through stabilization and intramolecular co-pigmentation
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. A 5,3'-acyltransferase contributes to diacylated delphinidin-based anthocyanins in gentian. 2. Inhibiting this acylating activity reduced intramolecular co-pigmentation. 3. This shifted flowers toward a red-purple color.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes blue coloration by enabling anthocyanin acylation and stabilizing intramolecular co-pigmentation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Inhibition of acylating activity summarized from cited study
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Specific gene symbol not provided in the review text.

Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Transcriptional activation as part of MBW complex
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Specialized MYB activity is usually the limiting determinant of anthocyanin accumulation in specific cells; Rosea1 is a powerful activator used for metabolic engineering
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: Genes encoding enzymes committed to anthocyanin biosynthesis; transporters and transport-associated proteins for vacuolar sequestration
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Activates anthocyanin biosynthetic pathway genes when co-expressed with bHLH partner; can induce high expression of pathway and transport genes
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: MBW complex with bHLH and WD-repeat proteins
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Pigmentation; antioxidant activity; associated dietary health benefits; extends shelf life and disease resistance in engineered tomato
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Combined with Delila, strongly increases anthocyanin accumulation to very high levels in tomato fruit and plant cell cultures.
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Rosea1 from snapdragon, together with Delila, enabled strong anthocyanin accumulation in tomato fruit. 2. Expression from the tomato fruit-specific E8 promoter produced purple tomatoes with about 3% dry weight anthocyanins and no yield penalty. 3. The engineered tomatoes had extended shelf life and reduced susceptibility to Botrytis cinerea. 4. The same regulatory strategy drove exceptionally high and stable anthocyanin production in tobacco and tomato suspension cultures.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transgenic co-expression; promoter engineering; anthocyanin metabolite profiling; disease/shelf-life phenotyping; nutritional intervention in mice
Regulation_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Tomato E8 promoter, fruit-specific; CaMV 35S promoter, constitutive
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Fruit ripening stage (breaker); postharvest infection by Botrytis cinerea
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: No yield penalty reported when expression was delayed using the E8 promoter
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: High-value regulator for engineering anthocyanin-enriched fruits and cell cultures; promoter choice is critical to avoid developmental penalties.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Monocot and dicot anthocyanin MYBs are not generally interchangeable; Rosea1 is a dicot regulator from snapdragon.

Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Transcriptional activation as part of MBW complex
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Acts as essential MBW partner; co-expression with MYB can maximize anthocyanin output
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: Anthocyanin biosynthetic genes and associated transport functions through MBW complex
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Co-activates anthocyanin biosynthetic pathway genes with MYB partners
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: MBW complex with MYB and WD-repeat proteins
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Pigmentation; antioxidant activity; associated dietary health benefits; shelf-life extension and disease resistance in engineered tomato
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: With Rosea1, enables very high anthocyanin accumulation in tomato fruit and cultured cells.
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Delila from snapdragon functions with Rosea1 to induce the anthocyanin pathway. 2. Co-expression under the E8 promoter produced purple tomatoes with high anthocyanin levels and no yield penalty. 3. Co-expression under CaMV 35S promoted strong anthocyanin production in suspension cultures. 4. High anthocyanin levels correlated with improved shelf life and reduced Botrytis susceptibility in tomato fruit.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transgenic co-expression; anthocyanin profiling; shelf-life and pathogen assays
Regulation_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Tomato E8 promoter, fruit-specific; CaMV 35S promoter, constitutive
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Fruit ripening stage (breaker); postharvest infection by Botrytis cinerea
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: No yield penalty reported under fruit-specific expression
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful bHLH partner for stacking with anthocyanin MYB activators to maximize production.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: bHLH proteins often have broader developmental roles, but in engineering systems co-expression with MYB boosts output strongly.

Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Usually the principal, cell-type-specific determinant and most common limiting regulator of anthocyanin accumulation
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: Genes encoding enzymes committed to anthocyanin biosynthesis
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Activate anthocyanin pathway genes and influence target specificity of MBW complex
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: MBW complex with bHLH and WDR proteins
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Pigmentation and associated health-promoting antioxidant traits
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Activation of these MYBs can ectopically induce anthocyanin accumulation in many tissues.
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. R2R3MYB proteins are the major specialized regulators determining anthocyanin accumulation patterns. 2. Their expression level usually explains differences in flower color intensity and pattern. 3. They are key tools for engineering anthocyanin biosynthesis in plants.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Review synthesis of genetic and transgenic evidence across species
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included because review centrally summarizes this regulator class as a primary engineering lever; not a single specific gene.

Regulation_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: bHLH, subgroup IIIf with conserved MYB-interaction N-terminal domain
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Essential MBW component; contribution becomes especially evident when co-overexpressed with MYB
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: Anthocyanin pathway genes in combination with MYB and WDR partners
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports strong induction of anthocyanin accumulation when paired with MYB regulators.
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. bHLH proteins are required partners in the MBW complex. 2. Their activity usually does not limit anthocyanin accumulation because they have broader functions and may already be expressed in tissues lacking anthocyanins. 3. Co-expression with MYB regulators can raise anthocyanin levels to those of very dark berries.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Review synthesis of mutant and overexpression evidence across species
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included as a summarized core regulator class rather than a single gene.

Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Obligate MBW component, though often constitutively expressed and not usually limiting
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Indirectly supports activation of anthocyanin pathway genes by stabilizing the MBW complex
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: MBW complex with MYB and bHLH proteins
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required component for activation of anthocyanin biosynthesis, but generally not the limiting determinant used for engineering.
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. WD-repeat proteins are part of the conserved MBW complex regulating anthocyanin biosynthesis. 2. They likely stabilize the complex and probably interact directly with bHLH proteins. 3. They are thought not to bind DNA and are often constitutively expressed.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Review synthesis of prior genetic and biochemical evidence
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included because the review emphasizes all three MBW components as obligately required.

Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Negative regulator that can repress anthocyanin biosynthesis non-cell autonomously
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: Anthocyanin MBW complexes via bHLH binding competition
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Represses activation of anthocyanin biosynthetic genes by blocking R2R3MYB-bHLH interactions
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Competes with activator MYBs for bHLH interaction
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Reduces anthocyanin biosynthesis and can act beyond the producing cell due to mobility.
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Small single-repeat MYB proteins act as negative regulators of anthocyanin biosynthesis. 2. They cannot bind DNA selectively but repress by competing with activating R2R3MYBs for bHLH binding. 3. Their small size allows cell-to-cell movement and non-cell-autonomous repression.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Review synthesis of prior functional studies
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: No specific gene name provided in this review text.
